evidence_quotes.quote: Many modern games are being sold as live-service or online-only
the permanent disabling or destruction of video games that have already been legally sold to consumers.
Prohibit companies from permanently disabling purchased digital products without offering a refund or offline alternative;

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, As a concerned EU citizen and consumer, I would like to thank you in efforts of advancing digital consumer proctection. Especially in areas of dark patterns, addictive designs and unfair commercial practices. I would like to respectfully suggest the addition of a new issue for consideration: the permanent disabling or destruction of video games that have already been legally sold to consumers. This issue has affected me personally. I have lost access to games such as LawBreakers, The Cycle, and Need for Speed: Worldall titles I enjoyed and supported as a paying customer. These games were either made unplayable due to server shutdowns or entirely removed without any meaningful alternative or refund. In each case, I was left with nothing, despite having paid for access. I am far from alone in this; many others have had similar experiences across a wide range of titles. The broader trend is deeply troubling. Many modern games are being sold as live-service or online-only, but come with no guarantee of long-term availability. The widespread concern over this issue is reflected in the Stop Killing Games movement, which has already gathered over one million signatures through a European Citizens Initiative. This level of public support clearly demonstrates that consumers across the EU care deeply about their right to retain access to the video games they purchase. A similar concern exists with professional software like Adobe Creative Suite and Autodesk Maya, which now rely on constant online authentication. If servers are shut down, users may lose access to critical tools they've paid for. While unofficial workarounds exist, they are not legal or dependable solutions for professionals. This highlights the broader risk of losing access to purchased digital products beyond just video games. I believe this practice undermines both trust and fairness in the digital marketplace. It reflects a growing problem where consumers are losing access to digital products they have legally purchasedwhether thats entertainment like video games or essential professional software. Increasingly, these products require constant online authentication or server connectivity, meaning that once support ends or servers are shut down, users may be locked out entirely. While some may resort to unofficial workarounds, these are not legal, dependable, or appropriate solutions, especially for paying customers. Therefore, I urge the Commission to consider adding provisions that would: - Prohibit companies from permanently disabling purchased digital products without offering a refund or offline alternative; - Require transparency and end-of-life plans for online-only or authentication-dependent products; - Enshrine the consumers right to retain access to what theyve paid for, even after a product is delisted or official support ends. By addressing this issue within the framework of the Digital Fairness Act, the EU has an opportunity to set a global precedent for meaningful digital ownership and consumer protection. The growing shift toward digital-only services must not come at the expense of basic consumer rights. People should not lose access to what they have paid for simply because a company decides to discontinue support or shut down a server. Thank you for taking the time to consider this matter. I hope the Commission will take this opportunity to ensure fair treatment for digital consumers across Europe and to help preserve both access and accountability in our increasingly digital world. Sincerely, Piotr Dziubek Citizen of Poland.
